symbols: crown placed on the child king — the restoration of the Davidic dynasty after Athaliah's usurpation
temple setting — the coronation happens in God's house, establishing divine sanction for the rightful king
kneeling crowd — the people's recognition of the legitimate heir of David
soldiers with spears — the armed guard Jehoiada assembled to protect the boy king during the dangerous transfer of power
figure in distress (Athaliah) — the usurper's terror at the revelation of the living heir she tried to destroy
description: A large-scale historical painting set within a monumental temple interior with massive stone pillars and stepped platforms. At the right, on an elevated platform, a young boy in royal red robes sits or stands, being crowned or presented, with a bearded man in priestly white garments beside him. Armed soldiers with spears and helmets stand guard on the steps. In the foreground and middle ground, a large crowd of people in varied ancient garments kneels, bows, and gestures in agitation. A female figure in green appears to be in distress, falling or being restrained at center-left.
theological_description: This painting depicts the dramatic coronation of seven-year-old Joash as king of Judah, as narrated in 2 Kings 11 and 2 Chronicles 23. After Queen Athaliah, daughter of Ahab and Jezebel, massacred the royal family to seize the throne, the infant Joash was secretly hidden in the temple by his aunt Jehosheba and the priest Jehoiada for six years. When the time was right, Jehoiada orchestrated a carefully planned coup: he armed the temple guards, brought forth the hidden prince, placed the crown upon his head, anointed him, and the people clapped and cried, 'Long live the king!' (2 Kings 11:12). When Athaliah heard the noise and rushed in, she tore her robes and cried 'Treason! Treason!' before being seized and executed. The theological significance is immense: this moment preserves the Davidic line through which the Messiah would come. God's covenant with David — 'Your throne shall be established forever' (2 Samuel 7:16) — hung by a thread during Athaliah's reign, and the preservation of Joash demonstrated that no human scheme can thwart God's redemptive purposes. Jehoiada the priest functions as both spiritual and political liberator, a type of the priestly-kingly restoration that finds its ultimate fulfillment in Christ, who is both priest and king of the everlasting Davidic kingdom.

scripture_refs.reason: The direct narrative of Jehoiada crowning Joash and the overthrow of Athaliah in the temple
The parallel account of the coronation of Joash in the Chronicles, with fuller details of the temple ceremony
The preservation of Joash fulfills God's covenant with David that his throne would be established forever
The backstory of Athaliah's massacre of the royal seed and the hiding of Joash by Jehosheba
The installation of the Lord's anointed king on Zion despite the rage of enemies parallels the coronation of Joash against Athaliah's opposition
